tldr: Teams leave Datadog when the per-host bill scales faster than the value they get from 700+ integrations. Better Stack offers a simpler, lower-cost path centered on logs, uptime, and on-call.
why_switch: Datadog peak-host pricing locks in costs after scale-down
Lower predictable bills with freemium and metered overages
Simpler product surface focused on logs and uptime
Lower lock-in: REST APIs and standard log shippers
Built-in on-call and incident response in one tool
export_method: Use Datadog's official APIs and account UI to extract data — there are public APIs for monitors, dashboards, logs, metrics, synthetics, and users. For dashboards and monitors the simplest path is to export their JSON definitions through the UI or API. For historical logs, use the log query export and pull results in batches; for metrics, use the metrics query API to pull time-series into local CSV/JSON. The Terraform provider for Datadog can also serialize most config (monitors, dashboards, SLOs) into source-controlled definitions, which is the cleanest way to capture state before cutover. Pull on-call schedules and incident workflows separately from those modules. Save raw exports to object storage so you have an immutable archive — Datadog log retention windows mean older data may be unrecoverable later.
import_method: In Better Stack, set up sources for each application/service to receive logs, then point your existing log shippers (Vector, Fluent Bit, Logstash, syslog, native SDKs) at the new ingest endpoints. Recreate uptime monitors and heartbeats in the Better Stack UI or via their API. On-call schedules, escalation policies, and incident routing are configured under the Incident Management section. For dashboards, rebuild key views manually — the data models differ enough that automated dashboard JSON conversion isn't practical. Run both systems in parallel for at least 1-2 weeks so you can validate alert parity and adjust thresholds against real traffic before cutting Datadog agents.

concept_mapping.note: Use Vector or Fluent Bit as a vendor-neutral bridge
Multi-step checks may not have parity

gotchas: Historical Datadog data outside retention window cannot be recovered after cancellation — archive proactively
APM/distributed-tracing parity may be incomplete; benchmark trace coverage before cutover
Custom metric cardinality that worked in Datadog can blow up costs elsewhere — review tag explosions
Per-host Datadog billing keeps charging at peak even after you scale down; cancel only after dual-run is verified
Alert noise will differ between platforms; expect a tuning period of 1-2 weeks
Compliance/retention requirements may need separate cold storage outside the new vendor

email.subject: Data export request and account sunset — please confirm timeline
email.body:
Hi Datadog team,

We are migrating off Datadog and would like to extract a complete export of our account data in machine-readable formats (JSON or CSV). Specifically we need: monitor and alert definitions, dashboard JSON, synthetic test configs, log archives within current retention, custom metric series, on-call schedules, user/team/role config, and tag metadata.

We are targeting a 14-day window to complete the export and validate it against our new platform. Please confirm:

1. Which exports require a support ticket vs. self-service via API/UI.
2. Whether log and metric history can be archived to our S3 bucket before cancellation.
3. The billing implications of downgrading hosts during the dual-run period and the exact sunset date once we cancel.

Thanks,
Your customer
